title: Stable Merge Sort with Incorrect Edge Handling

statement.markdown: Given an array of integers, implement a stable merge sort algorithm. The algorithm should be able to handle edge cases such as empty arrays, arrays with a single element, and arrays with duplicate elements. However, the provided implementation contains a bug that affects its ability to handle edge cases correctly.
statement.inputFormat: Input will be an array of integers, e.g. [1, 5, 2, 8, 3, 9, 4, 7, 6].
statement.outputFormat: The output should be a sorted array of integers, e.g. [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9].
statement.notes: The algorithm should be stable, meaning that equal elements maintain their original order.

hints.text: Re-examine the merge function and identify the edge case that causes the incorrect result.

editorial.title: Correct Implementation of Stable Merge Sort
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved by implementing a stable merge sort algorithm that handles edge cases correctly.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force solution would be to use a simple bubble sort algorithm, but this will have a time complexity of O(n^2).
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution would be to use a merge sort algorithm, which has a time complexity of O(n log n).
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to implement a stable merge sort algorithm that handles edge cases correctly.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The algorithm is correct because it recursively divides the array into smaller subarrays until each subarray contains only one element. Then it merges the subarrays in a stable manner to produce the final sorted array.
